metadata.title: Research the Three Biases That Make Screening Look Better Than It Is with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The claim that survival can rise while no one lives longer is the paradox I want to break apart with a worked case. Take lead-time, length-time, and overdiagnosis one at a time. Can you (1) build me a numeric toy example for each bias showing five-year survival inflating while the death date stays fixed, (2) explain why a randomized trial measuring disease-specific mortality is immune to all three at once, and (3) contrast that with how the NLST's twenty-percent mortality reduction was actually measured?

props.command: The claim that survival can rise while no one lives longer is the paradox I want to break apart with a worked case. Take lead-time, length-time, and overdiagnosis one at a time. Can you (1) build me a numeric toy example for each bias showing five-year survival inflating while the death date stays fixed, (2) explain why a randomized trial measuring disease-specific mortality is immune to all three at once, and (3) contrast that with how the NLST's twenty-percent mortality reduction was actually measured?
props.segment: Research the Three Biases That Make

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Survival went up. No one lived longer.

lines: Survival went up. No one lived longer.

beats.narration_text: A new screening test boasts eighty percent five-year survival versus forty-five for symptom-detected cases. Nearly double. But no one in the screened group lived a day longer. How can survival improve while lifespan stays identical? The answer is three biases that are not fraud — they are artifacts of the measurement itself.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — problem card: 80% vs 45% survival with a wedge showing this proves nothing about lifespan

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to explain all three biases with a real published example of each where the bias inflated apparent screening benefit.

props.command:
claude "Explain lead-time bias, length-time bias, and
overdiagnosis with one published real-world example
of each where the bias inflated apparent screening
benefit. For each, describe one study design feature
that would allow a researcher to rule it out.
Synthesize into a brief that a clinician could use
to evaluate any new screening claim."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the output. The script structures the three-bias framework as a comparison table — apparent benefit, the mechanism behind it, the reality it hides, and the study design that exposes it.

props.code:
# screening_biases.py -- Claude Code output
BIASES = [
  {"name": "LEAD-TIME",
   "apparent": "Better 5-year survival after diagnosis",
   "reality":  "Diagnosed earlier — same death date",
   "example":  "Prostate PSA: 5-yr survival 99%, no OS gain",
   "fix":      "Randomized trial measuring disease-specific mortality"},
  {"name": "LENGTH-TIME",
   "apparent": "Screened patients do better on average",
   "reality":  "Screen catches slow-growing tumors preferentially",
   "example":  "Thyroid cancer screening: incidence +300%, mortality flat",
   "fix":      "Compare interval cancers vs screen-detected cancers"},
  {"name": "OVERDIAGNOSIS",
   "apparent": "More cancers detected = more lives saved",
   "reality":  "Some tumors would never have harmed the patient",
   "example":  "DCIS: treatment without mortality endpoint evidence",
   "fix":      "Active surveillance arm; long-term mortality endpoint"},
]
for b in BIASES:
    print(f"[{b['name']}] {b['apparent']} => {b['reality']}")

beats.narration_text: The table. Lead-time: you are diagnosed earlier, so your five-year survival clock starts earlier — the death date does not move. Length-time: screening finds slow tumors first because they are there longer to find. Overdiagnosis: some detected cancers would never have caused symptoms. Each inflates the benefit signal without changing mortality.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: B04_ThreeBiasTable — three-row table building left-to-right with apparent benefit and reality columns

beats.narration_text: Add a fourth row: the study design that would be immune to all three biases simultaneously.

props.command:
claude "Add a fourth row to the bias table:
'What would settle it' — the single study design
that would be immune to lead-time bias, length-time
bias, and overdiagnosis simultaneously. Is the NLST
an example of that design?"

beats.narration_text: The answer: a randomized controlled trial measuring disease-specific mortality, with a no-screening control arm and follow-up long enough to capture overdiagnosis. The NLST showed a twenty percent reduction in lung-cancer mortality — because it measured mortality, not survival time from diagnosis.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — fourth row added: randomized mortality-endpoint trial; NLST annotation as example

beats.narration_text: Survival time from diagnosis is not lifespan. The gap between those two statements is where most screening marketing lives. The three biases are not dishonesty — they are consequences of measuring the wrong endpoint. The only endpoint that settles the question is disease-specific mortality in a randomized trial.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: survival ≠ lifespan; only mortality endpoint settles screening claims

beats.narration_text: When evaluating any screening claim, ask one question first: does this study report disease-specific mortality or survival-from-diagnosis? If it reports survival, the three biases are in play. Discount accordingly and look for the randomized mortality endpoint before drawing conclusions.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps: ask mortality vs survival; check for RCT design; apply the three-bias filter

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Survival time from diagnosis is not lifespan. The gap between those two statements is where most screening marketing lives. The three biases are not dishonesty — they are consequences of measuring the wrong endpoint. The only endpoint that settles the question is disease-specific mortality in a randomized trial. When evaluating any screening claim, ask one question first: does this study report disease-specific mortality or survival-from-diagnosis? If it reports survival, the three biases are in play. Discount accordingly and look for the randomized mortality endpoint before drawing conclusions.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research the Three Biases That Make Screening Look Better Than It Is w] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
